summary: A lightweight, single-binary Python CLI tool that reads CSV files line-by-line, validates structure, and serializes rows into JSON (either to stdout or a specified file), with robust error reporting and minimal dependencies.

components.name: CLI Entrypoint
components.responsibility: Parses command-line arguments, orchestrates execution flow, handles top-level error reporting and exit codes.
interfaces: accepts --input, --output, --pretty, --help; emits exit code 0 on success, non-zero on failure

components.name: CSV Reader
components.responsibility: Streams CSV rows incrementally using csv.DictReader; detects parsing errors (e.g., inconsistent columns, encoding issues) and reports line numbers and context.
interfaces: reads from file handle; yields dict per row; raises CsvValidationError with line number and message

components.name: JSON Serializer
components.responsibility: Converts list of row dicts into valid JSON; supports compact and pretty-printed output; handles JSON serialization errors (e.g., non-serializable types).
interfaces: accepts list of dicts and indent parameter; returns bytes or str; raises JsonSerializationError

components.name: Output Writer
components.responsibility: Writes JSON output to stdout or filesystem; ensures atomic write when writing to file (via temp file + rename); handles permission and disk-full errors.
interfaces: accepts bytes payload and optional output path; writes and flushes; raises IOError subclasses

deployment_strategy: Distribute as executable script or installable package via pip (setup.py/pyproject.toml); no server or runtime dependencies beyond CPython.
security_considerations: Input file paths are validated to prevent directory traversal (e.g., normalize and verify input is within allowed scope); no code execution or eval usage; output file paths are sanitized before write; no sensitive data handling assumptions — tool treats all CSV content as untrusted but does not process it beyond parsing/serialization.
scalability_notes: Memory-efficient streaming: processes CSV row-by-row without loading entire file into memory; suitable for multi-gigabyte files given sufficient disk I/O; performance scales linearly with file size; no parallelism needed for typical use cases; further optimization possible via mmap for ultra-large files, but out of scope per constraints.
